title: Lesson 249
transcript: you We just talked about BCP plans and DRP plans. Now, let's talk about the numbers that drive the creation of these plans. RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD. Now, before I get into explaining exactly how we come up with these numbers and what they mean, It's very important to understand one thing about them, and that is all of these numbers are a measurement of time. Minutes, seconds, hours, days, weeks, months, years. Every single one of these numbers, RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD, they are all measurements of time. So let's get into explaining what each of these numbers means. And to explain this, we have a little diagram here. And this diagram shows time going forward. So we're starting off as business as usual. Everything is working perfectly. And now time starts rolling. And the first thing that happens is disaster strikes. A disaster has occurred. The first measurement, RPO, is what's known as the maximum tolerable downtime. And it's actually a measurement of how much data you're willing to lose as a measurement of... time. So how many hours worth of data? How many seconds worth of data? How many minutes worth of data? RPO is a measurement of maximum tolerable data loss as a measurement of time. And what's important to understand about RPO is that it's actually something that you have to plan for ahead of the disaster occurring. Because if you've said your RPO is only 10 minutes, you're only willing to lose 10 minutes worth of data, That means you have to architect your systems in a particular way. You can't, for instance, just be doing a nightly backup every 24 hours because then what would your RPO be? What would your maximum tolerable data loss be if you were only backing up every night? Well, it's up to... 23 hours and 59 minutes. So RPO is all about how you architect, design your systems before a disaster occurs to only lose a certain amount of data as a measurement of time. Now, the next number, our TO, Recovery Time Objective, is a measurement of how long does it take from when the disaster occurs to when you're up and running at some defined service level. So for instance, if the disaster was your primary data center blew up, Your recovery point would be how long does it take to get up and running in your backup data center. And also RTO is a measurement of how long does it take you to get to that defined service level. The next measurement, WRT, Work Recovery Time, is a measurement of how long does it take to get from your recovery point all the way back to business as usual. And WRT is really a measurement of how long does it take you to do all your validation and checking to make sure that all the systems that you're bringing back online are working correctly. The final number is what's known as the MTD. And this is the most important number. The MTD is your maximum tolerable downtime. It's the maximum amount of time that a business process or system can be down before the business is out of business. And so, MTD is made up of your RTO and your WRT, or to put it another way, your RTO and your WRT together have to always be less than whatever your maximum tolerable downtime is. And we'll talk again about MTD when we talk about incident response, because MTD is a very important number that we use in that process for when we decide whether or not we're going to declare a disaster. And the final piece I'll mention here is the way that we come up with these numbers, the process we go through to come up with RPO, RTO, WRT, and MTD is the BIA, the Business Impact Assessment. And we'll talk about that one next.
